topic: Schumann needs conductive ceiling — dome's quarter-wave c/4f gives 9,572 km
change_summary: tldr_evidence grew from 337 to 629 chars (+86.6%); detail_evidence grew from 2,832 to 3,262 chars (+15.2%); sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-28T01:09:01Z; obe_skipped=2 (WIN-006 c6 2026-05-28 and WIN-004 c7 2026-05-29 both re-reviewed since audit). The c3 review's recommended tldr rewrite (hole 4) was applied — three cavity heights now enumerated — but introduced a misattribution and a mixed-base convention; see holes 1–2 below.
our_argument_summary: WIN-029 argues the dome's claim that Schumann resonance requires a conductive dome is refuted because (a) the ionosphere is conductive at 10^-4 to 10^-2 S/m; (b) the dome's c/4f formula is a quarter-wave linear-waveguide formula, not the spherical-shell eigenmode formula f_1 = c√2/(2πR) appropriate for a curved cavity, which gives ~10.59 Hz at Earth's radius — recovered to 7.83 Hz by finite ionospheric conductivity corrections involving NO free parameters; (c) the dome's three stated cavity heights (9,572, 9,086, 8,537 km) are mutually inconsistent; (d) the dome's own exponential cavity (linear-radial H_eff ≈ 3,400 km) gives ~22 Hz; (e) the observed Q-factor of 4–6 is incompatible with a metallic dome (Q > 1000 for cast copper).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py Domain 1 (live-power.txt) stores Predicted=7.83 Hz and Observed=7.83 Hz both hardcoded; status is computed as |pred-obs|/pred ≤ 5%, trivially PASS. Source listed as 'Tomsk SR / stable published value'. There is no live fetch of measured Schumann frequency — the comparison is identity of two static constants.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded both sides. The 'monitoring' is display-only.
data_sources_used: No external API for this WIN; cited source ('Tomsk SR') is published-literature constant
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 5% tolerance band is wider than necessary for an identity comparison and accommodates any future drift if either constant were ever wired in; currently unused.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the c3 review's code_analysis description matches current raw-text/monitor.py contents.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/08-live-power.txt: Domain 1 stores both Predicted=7.83 Hz and Observed=7.83 Hz as static constants; pred==obs to ≤5% trivially. No URL/API fetch for Schumann measurement. Tag inherited from c3 — re-validated against current raw-text/monitor.py.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Schumann (1952) predicted the resonance; Balser & Wagner (1962) measured 7.83 Hz; the value has been textbook physics for >60 years. The dome retains the same numerical value while swapping the physical mechanism (ionospheric cavity with finite conductivity → cast copper/bronze dome). No new numerical prediction is generated. Tag inherited from c3 — still accurate.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Balser & Wagner (J. Research NBS D, Radio Propagation 65D, 65–72, 1962) published the 7.83 Hz measurement before any dome-model code existed. Per Part 3.1b 'trivial-transcription test' the value is in undergraduate texts. Tag inherited from c3 — confirmed against the new detail_verdict_text framing (the post-hoc paragraph was strengthened with 'algebraically inverts a textbook quarter-wave formula' wording per c3 hole 3).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: c/4f is algebraic inversion from measured f to a cavity H — not a forward prediction from dome geometry. The resulting 9,572 km does not match any of the dome's three independent cavity-height parameters (firmament_height = 9,086 km; H(r=0) from exponential fit = 8,537 km; pole-to-apex from coordinate fit ≈ 9,086 km). Tag inherited from c3.

holes_found.description: TLDR final sentence misattributes the three percent figures to the spherical formula. Current text: 'Applying the correct spherical formula to the dome's own three stated cavity heights (9,572 km, 9,086 km, 8,537 km) gives discrepancies of 5.3%, 10.8%, and 6.4% respectively — no single dome geometry is self-consistent with the measurement.' But the spherical formula f_1 = c√(n(n+1))/(2πR) yields FREQUENCIES (about 7.05, 7.43, 7.91 Hz respectively for those three radii), not those percentage discrepancies. The 5.3% / 10.8% / 6.4% numbers are pairwise relative differences between the three heights themselves — independent of any formula application. The immediately-preceding sentence ('Applying the correct spherical formula gives Earth's radius, not a dome height') is the spherical-formula payoff; conflating the two arguments lets a careful reader notice a non-sequitur and dismiss the TLDR as confused.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the final TLDR sentence to separate the two arguments. Suggested: 'Separately, the dome's three stated cavity heights — 9,572 km (from c/4f), 9,086 km (the stated firmament_height), and 8,537 km (H(r=0) of the dome's own exponential fit) — are mutually inconsistent, with pairwise discrepancies of 5.3%, 6.4%, and 12.1%. No single dome geometry is self-consistent with the measurement.' This drops the misleading 'Applying the correct spherical formula' framing, gives each height a parenthetical provenance, and uses a consistent base=smaller convention (see hole 2).

holes_found.description: Mixed-base convention in the TLDR's three pairwise discrepancies. Current text gives 5.3%, 10.8%, and 6.4%. Computing: (9572−9086)/9086 = 5.35% [base=smaller=9086]; (9086−8537)/8537 = 6.43% [base=smaller=8537]; but 10.8% only works as (9572−8537)/9572 = 10.81% [base=LARGER=9572]; the base=smaller value is (9572−8537)/8537 = 12.12%. The 5.3% and 6.4% figures use base=smaller while 10.8% uses base=larger. The detail_evidence section already uses base=smaller for its 6.4% claim ('H(r=0) = 8,537 km from its own exponential formula, vs. firmament_height = 9,086 km from the model's stated core parameters — a 6.4% discrepancy never acknowledged'), so the TLDR is inconsistent with the body it summarizes.
holes_found.recommendation: Standardize on base=smaller in the TLDR: 5.3%, 6.4%, 12.1% (matches the detail_evidence convention). Alternatively standardize on base=larger: 5.1%, 6.0%, 10.8%. Either is defensible; mixing them is the issue. Combining with hole 1, the proposed replacement sentence already incorporates this fix.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN inconsistency with WIN-002 on H_eff value and resulting frequency. WIN-029 detail_evidence says 'The dome's own exponential cavity (H_eff ≈ 3,400 km linear radial average) gives ~22 Hz.' WIN-002 detail_evidence explicitly computes 'the linear radial average gives H_eff = (H₀·L/R)(1−e^(−R/L)) ≈ 3,316 km → f = c/(4 × 3,316) = 22.6 Hz.' Verified independently: with H₀=8537, L=8619, R=20015, the linear radial integral is 0.4265·8619·(1−e^(−2.322)) ≈ 0.4265·8619·0.9020 ≈ 3,316 km, and c/(4·3316) = 22.60 Hz. WIN-029's '3,400 km' is rounded too aggressively (off by 2.5%) and the resulting '~22 Hz' is fine at one significant figure but masks the precise 22.6 Hz that WIN-002 computes. A defender pointing at WIN-002 and asking 'which is it, 3,316 or 3,400?' has a small but real attack surface.
holes_found.recommendation: Align WIN-029's wording with WIN-002's computed values: replace '(H_eff ≈ 3,400 km linear radial average) gives ~22 Hz' with '(H_eff ≈ 3,316 km linear radial average; see WIN-002) gives ~22.6 Hz'. Adds 8 characters and ties the two WINs together explicitly.

holes_found.description: Carry-over from c3 hole 5 (still open): the Q-factor argument in detail_evidence has no citation. Current text: '...narrow peaks at exact harmonics rather than the broad resonances observed.' c3's recommendation was to add (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa, 2002; Sátori et al., 2005) to match the citation density elsewhere in the WIN. The detail_evidence has been edited since c3 (length grew from 2,832 to 3,262 chars) but the Q-factor passage was not patched. The 'Q > 1000 for cast copper / Q in the hundreds for bronze' wording (c3 hole 6) WAS applied, so the editor visited this passage — the citation gap is now a known-but-not-fixed gap, which is worse than a never-noticed gap.
holes_found.recommendation: Append references in the Q-factor sentence: '...narrow peaks at exact harmonics rather than the broad resonances observed (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa, 2002; Sátori et al., 2005).' Note: no matching open ISS found in open-issues.json — decider should either patch directly or create an ISS so the carry-over is tracked.

holes_found.description: Averaging-convention choice not acknowledged. WIN-029 detail_evidence picks the linear-radial average ('H_eff ≈ 3,400 km linear radial average') without noting that WIN-002 explicitly labels the area-weighted average as 'proper for a disc' (WIN-002 detail_evidence: 'the area-weighted average (proper for a disc) gives H_eff ≈ 2,140 km → 35 Hz'). A dome defender could complain: 'You chose the dome-favorable averaging in WIN-029 to argue ~22 Hz, when by your OWN definition in WIN-002 the proper averaging gives 35 Hz — why pick the smaller number unless you wanted to flatter the dome?' The current WIN-029 wording is technically correct (linear-radial IS one defensible averaging) but rhetorically vulnerable. The c3 hole 1 recommendation offered a fallback phrasing — 'The dome's exponential cavity yields ~22 Hz under the most dome-favourable averaging convention' — which would preempt this.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a parenthetical: '(linear radial; WIN-002 notes that the area-weighted average — more appropriate for a disc — gives 35 Hz, but we use the dome-favorable value here to avoid the appearance of cherry-picking)', or (b) adopt the neutral phrasing from c3 hole 1: 'The dome's exponential cavity yields ~22 Hz under the most dome-favorable averaging convention; the area-weighted average (proper for a disc, per WIN-002) gives 35 Hz.' Option (b) is shorter and more directly preempts the defense.

stronger_arguments.description: The Schumann Q-factor argument can be tightened with a quantitative skin-depth calculation. Surface resistance Rs = √(πfμ₀/σ); for copper at 7.83 Hz, Rs ≈ √(π × 7.83 × 4π×10^-7 / 5.96×10^7) ≈ 2.2×10^-5 Ω/sq. Cavity Q ≈ 2πfL/R_eq ~ 10^3-10^4 depending on geometry — orders of magnitude above observed Q ≈ 4-6. Putting this short derivation into the detail_evidence (or a footnote) converts the current qualitative claim ('Q > 1000 for cast copper') into a defensible computation.
sources: Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, Resonances in the Earth-Ionosphere Cavity

stronger_arguments.description: The c/4f-vs-spherical-shell distinction can be made more rhetorically forceful: the quarter-wave formula DESCRIBES a closed pipe, where wave nodes pin to the ends. A spherical cavity is fundamentally different topology — there are no 'ends.' The dome's c/4f formula imports closed-pipe topology onto a curved-shell geometry; that's not just 'wrong formula,' it's a category error. One sentence to this effect would land harder than the current 'is not a linear waveguide — it is a curved shell over a disc.'
sources: Schumann 1952 original derivation; any standard electrodynamics text on cavity resonances (Jackson §8)

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome author is correct that Schumann resonance REQUIRES a conductive boundary at some altitude — a vacuum cavity is impossible (data/wins.json:029.detail_evidence). The reasoning that 'some conductive ceiling is needed' is sound electromagnetism. The author is also correct that 7.83 Hz × λ ≈ a wavelength of c/f ≈ 38,000 km that bears a specific algebraic relationship to typical cavity dimensions (data/wins.json:029 implicit in c/4f manipulation). And the author has noticed something real about the dome's three independent cavity-height parameters not agreeing — a self-contradiction internal to the dome model that is worth confronting directly (data/wins.json:029.detail_evidence, '...two internally inconsistent firmament heights...').
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The conductive-boundary requirement is satisfied by the ionosphere — 10^-4 to 10^-2 S/m at 60-300 km altitude per CHAMP satellite measurements cited in data/wins.json:029.detail_evidence — making the dome unnecessary. The c/4f manipulation isn't a 'prediction' from dome geometry; it's algebraic inversion of the measured frequency to back-solve a length (made explicit in the detail_verdict_text post-hoc paragraph). And the dome's noticing of internal inconsistency is more incriminating than exculpatory: a dome that yields three different cavity heights for the same purported physical structure (9,572 from c/4f; 9,086 from firmament_height; 8,537 from H(r=0) of exponential fit, per data/wins.json:029.detail_evidence) cannot also claim self-consistent geometric derivation. The kernel — 'we need a conductive ceiling' — is fine; the dome adds nothing the ionosphere doesn't already provide.

advocate_mode.best_defense: The strongest dome-defender rebuttal is to attack the framing 'correct spherical formula' as question-begging. The defender says: 'You demand the dome's geometry satisfy the spherical Schumann formula f_1 = c√(n(n+1))/(2πR) — but the dome is explicitly NOT a sphere. By your own internal-consistency principle, you should evaluate the dome model on ITS OWN equations, not the equations of the model you're already committed to. The dome's c/4f formula is the dome's claim; the spherical formula is the globe's claim. Comparing them and concluding 'the spherical one matches Earth's radius, so the dome is wrong' is exactly the default-favoritism your methodology rejects (Part 4-ish principles). Furthermore, the percent-discrepancies between the dome's three cavity heights aren't a refutation either — every model has parameter-estimation noise; the dome simply hasn't pinned down which height is the canonical one, and the 5–12% spread is comparable to ionospheric-altitude uncertainty in the standard cavity (which is also given as a range, ~60–100 km). You're holding the dome to a tighter consistency standard than you hold the standard model.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The defense is partially preempted but could be neutralized more cleanly. The current detail_evidence already says 'The author's claim: H = c/(4f)... uses a quarter-wave linear waveguide formula, not a resonant cavity formula' and explains why c/4f is structurally wrong for a curved cavity. But the response to 'evaluate the dome on its own equations' could be sharper: ADD a sentence stating that the dome's c/4f is not a prediction at all — it's an algebraic inversion of the measured 7.83 Hz to back-solve a length, with no independent derivation from dome geometry (this is already in the detail_verdict_text, but moving a single sentence into detail_evidence at the point of first mention would close the gap). For the 'parameter-estimation noise' part: ADD that the ionospheric altitude range (~60–100 km, 40 km spread) is OBSERVED — measured by multiple independent satellites — while the dome's three cavity heights (8,537 / 9,086 / 9,572 km, a 1,035 km spread) are all FITTED PARAMETERS the dome model itself generates, so they should converge if the model is self-consistent.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: (a) WIN-002 says H_eff = 3,316 km linear-radial → 22.6 Hz; WIN-029 says H_eff ≈ 3,400 km linear-radial → ~22 Hz. Same calculation, different rounding. See hole 3. (b) WIN-002 explicitly labels the area-weighted average as 'proper for a disc' but WIN-029 silently uses the linear-radial average and reports its result as definitive. See hole 5. (c) WIN-013/014 — the broader 'firmament_height parameter is unconstrained' findings — are consistent with WIN-029's three-different-cavity-heights argument; no contradiction there, but WIN-029 could cross-reference more explicitly to strengthen the self-contradiction case.

claims_checked: H_eff linear-radial for H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619), R=20015: ≈ 3,316 km
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) WIN-029's H_eff = 3,400 km is rounded too aggressively from the true 3,316 km — mismatches WIN-002's exact computation. (2) TLDR's '10.8%' uses base=larger while '5.3%' and '6.4%' use base=smaller — inconsistent convention. Numerically the 10.8% is correct under its (unstated) base choice; it's the mixing that's wrong.

deeper_analysis: The WIN-029 verdict (Self-Contradicted) holds firmly — three internally inconsistent cavity heights, a back-solved 'prediction' from measured frequency, and a Q-factor incompatible with the proposed boundary all converge on the same conclusion. The c3 review's targeted recommendations on the symmetry defense (hole 2), the 'algebraically inverts' wording (hole 3), and the 'Q > 1000 / hundreds' Q-factor numbers (hole 6) have all been applied cleanly. The c3 tldr-rewrite recommendation (hole 4) was applied in spirit but introduced two new defects in the very sentence the rewrite targeted: a non-sequitur attaching the pairwise-discrepancy numbers to 'Applying the correct spherical formula', and a mixed-base percentage convention. Both are confined to one sentence in the TLDR; the body text (detail_evidence) is correct. The unaddressed c3 carry-over (Q-factor citation, hole 5) is a clean two-word patch the decider should be able to handle as a minor_edit. Cross-WIN consistency with WIN-002 reveals that two related WINs report the same calculation with different rounding (3,316 vs 3,400; 22.6 vs 22 Hz); aligning them would strengthen both. The advocate-mode best defense (rated 3/5) survives partially because the 'evaluate the dome on its own equations' framing is rhetorically sticky — the existing 'algebraically inverts' framing in detail_verdict_text preempts it but doesn't appear in detail_evidence where the c/4f formula is first introduced; moving that idea earlier would close the gap.

citations_verified: Sentman 1990 — Mod-amp variation of Schumann resonances (real paper, ionospheric conductivity profile dependence)
Mushtak & Williams 2002 — explicit Schumann eigenmode + ionospheric conductivity model (Earth-ionosphere cavity)
Schumann 1952 — original derivation of the resonance, Z. Naturforschung 7a
Balser & Wagner 1962 — first measurement, J. Research NBS D, Radio Propagation 65D, 65–72
CHAMP satellite — ionospheric conductivity measurements, GFZ Potsdam (URL in detail_evidence resolves)
citations_unchecked: The two suggested Q-factor citations (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002; Sátori et al. 2005) are well-known in the Schumann literature and likely fine but not verified to a specific DOI in this review.

summary_for_decider: WIN-029 verdict (Self-Contradicted) holds. Five findings — two moderate (TLDR misattribution + mixed-base discrepancy convention, both confined to a single TLDR sentence; combine into one patch); three minor (H_eff rounding mismatch with WIN-002, carry-over Q-factor citation from c3 hole 5, averaging-convention defense surface). The Q-factor citation gap is a carry-over but no matching open ISS exists — decider should patch directly OR open an ISS to track. None of these change the verdict; all are tractable as minor_edit patches. The c3 carry-over field (unresolved_prior_cycle) is empty because no ISS-NNNN exists for that hole; if decider creates one, future cycles can use the structured field.
